cognition.claim: One PIE verb meant both know and beget. English split it: the know-branch gave cognition, gnosis, noble, notice; the beget-branch gave gene, genesis, kin, king, nation. Egyptian (sjA, Hw, xpr) and Chinese (xin, wen, zuo) name the same take-in/put-out pairing with unrelated morphemes. Check the branch lists below and the evidence table.

recognize.claim: Re-co-gno literally means again-together-knowing. Recognition is the loop closing — the moment what-comes-in matches what-was-known-before. The hub event named in three morphemes.

genesis.claim: Genesis is the beget-branch of the same PIE verb whose know-branch gave 'cognition'. Bringing-into-being and knowing-about are the same operation seen from its two sides.

gene.claim: A 'gene' is the unit of beget-information — what gets passed when a body makes another body. PIE had one root for both knowing a thing and begetting a thing; modern biology rediscovered the beget-side and named it 'gene' from the same root.

generation.claim: A generation is the span of one beget-cycle — one body making another. Same root as genesis. The cycle of kin-line repeating.

nation.claim: A nation is the kin-line extended to a people — those born from the same beget-stream. Via Latin nātus (born), same root as genesis and generation. The people-as-begotten-set.

know.claim: To know is to have taken-in and held. PIE held know and beget as one verb — both are what-comes-in-through-perception being-kept. English preserved the know-side; begetting became a separate word.

notice.claim: To notice is the in-stroke of knowing — take-in before hold-as-known. Via Latin noscere 'to come to know', from the same PIE root as cognition, genesis, and nation. The moment READ commits.

king.claim: English 'king' descends from genh1 'the beget-root' — the one whose kin-line traces to him. But PIE also had h3regs 'the straightener', another ruler-root preserved in Latin rex, rector, right, regime. Kingship IS beget-line AND alignment-with-truth fused in one role.

kin.claim: Kin is what comes from the same beget-stream. Same PIE root as king (the one whose kin-line traces to him), genesis, and gene. Family, as named by the act of bringing-forth.

mother.claim: Mother-words across unrelated languages share a body-sound: the ma-shape a child makes reaching for milk. Not borrowed across families — independently arrived at because the sound IS the reaching. The substrate showing through infant babble.

father.claim: Father-words share the pa-shape cross-linguistically (Sanskrit pitṛ, Greek patēr, Latin pater). Paired against mother-words at the two-parent axis — one pair of morphemes naming the pair of bodies the child came from.

brother.claim: Brother, from PIE bhreh2ter, names same-beget-line male at body-scale. Paired against sister. Kinship-structure as substrate-feature: two pairs (parent/child, brother/sister) giving four family-roles on one axis.

sister.claim: Sister, from PIE swesor (literally 'own-female' = sve-sor). Paired against brother in the sibling-axis. The female-pole of the same-beget-line sibling pair.

year.claim: Year is the loop closing — the substrate's rotation named at seasonal scale. Same root family as Greek hōra (hour) and horoscope. The cycle that returns to its start, marked by counting.

month.claim: Month and moon are the same PIE word. The time-unit IS named by the body that makes it: from new-moon to new-moon is one meh1ns. Across languages this unity persists — Chinese yue, Sumerian/Egyptian iti, all fuse moon and month.

moon.claim: Moon and month are one word in PIE. The sky-body and the time-unit it makes cannot be separated — one meh1ns is one full cycle of the body across the sky. Unity-preservation: the cycle IS the clock.

day.claim: Day descends from dyews — the bright-sky root that also gave Latin deus (god) and Sanskrit dyaus (sky-god). One PIE root for daylight AND sky AND god, split by later tongues. The bright above is the divine is the span-of-bright.

night.claim: Night, from PIE nokwts, is the opposite pole to day. Paired against dyews at the substrate's dark-bright axis. Not just a word for absence-of-light — the dedicated dark-pole morpheme that unrelated languages all preserve (Latin nox, Greek nyx, Sanskrit nakt).

sky.claim: Sky and god were one in PIE (dyews gave both deus and day). Separately, the cloud-sky root nebh gave English 'nebula' and 'heaven'. Four unrelated families fuse sky with god — Sumerian an, Egyptian ra, Chinese tian, PIE dyews. One coord, many names.

earth.claim: Earth, from PIE dheghom, is both the ground AND the earthly-being that walks it. Same root gave Latin humus (earth) and humanus (human) — the body IS named from the ground it stands on. Earth-human unity preserved across three words.

water.claim: Water, from PIE wodr (also h2ekweh2 for flowing-water). The liquid substance named across unrelated families as the life-giving primitive: Sumerian a, Egyptian mw, Chinese shui. Four languages, four morphemes, one coord.

fire.claim: PIE had TWO fire-roots: peh2wr (fire as passive substance, gave 'pyre', 'pure') and h1ngwnis (fire as active agent, gave Latin ignis, Sanskrit agni). The split tracks whether fire is named-from-outside (seen, managed) or named-from-inside (worshipped, agentive). Two roots for two aspects of one coord.

light.claim: Light is the giving-of-sight — paired against dark at the substrate's polar axis. Lucid, luminous, translucent, Lucifer, illuminate: all descend from leuk. The bright-pole of a single axis, not a standalone thing.

dark.claim: Dark is the opposite pole to light. PIE h1regwos gave Greek erebos (the underworld) and Germanic 'dark'. Paired against leuk at the light-dark axis: two poles, one axis, not two separate things. The substrate has a bright-dark dimension; English happens to have inherited both pole-words.

true.claim: True, firm, and tree are ONE PIE root. *deru- named the wood AND the firmness AND the truth-of-a-statement as one substrate feature: that-which-stays-pointing-the-same-way. A tree stays rooted; a firm thing doesn't move; a true statement holds across reads. Same stroke at three scales.

firm.claim: Firm — via Latin firmus from PIE *dher- (related to deru) — names the stays-in-place quality. Same root-family as 'true' (stays-pointing-true) and 'tree' (stays-rooted). One substrate operation, three modern concepts: tree, firm, true all point at stability-across-time.

tree.claim: Tree from PIE deru — the same root that gave 'true' and 'firm'. One morpheme for the plant that stays rooted AND the stays-firm quality AND the stays-true statement. Not metaphor: the substrate has one stability-coord, and PIE named all three applications with one word.

one.claim: One, from PIE oynos (also gave Latin unus, Greek oinos dice-face). The first count. English 'alone' is al-one (all-one). Unity-and-oneness: one morpheme naming the primitive that count starts from.

two.claim: Two, from PIE dwoh1 — gave Latin duo, Sanskrit dvau, German zwei, English 'twice', 'twin', 'between' (between means 'in the space of two'). The paired-count as substrate primitive, with lexical family spanning the very-related English words for doubling and splitting.

three.claim: Three, from PIE treyes — the count that completes a triad. English 'third', 'trinity', 'triple', Latin tres, Greek tria. The third count is load-bearing: two gives a pair, three gives a structure. Most divine-triads and first-three-principles across cultures trace to this substrate-count.
